title: Preparing for Scrutiny: The Complete PE Due Diligence Checklist for ...
content:
# PE Due Diligence Checklist for Healthcare Practices

*Source: SovDoc | [Original Article](https://sovdoc.com/pe-due-diligence-checklist-healthcare/)*

---

## Overview

Due diligence is the most intensive phase of a PE transaction, occurring after initial presentations and Letters of Intent. This checklist serves as a strategic roadmap to validate practice value and build buyer confidence.

## Why Due Diligence Matters

A well-organized due diligence process achieves **three critical goals**:

1. **Validates Valuation** — Clean, organized data confirms financial and operational health supporting your valuation
2. **Builds Buyer Confidence** — Smooth process signals well-run practice, reduces buyer's risk perception, prevents last-minute price reduction attempts
3. **Speeds Up Timeline** — Being prepared reduces back-and-forth, accelerating time to closing

> *"Having your own advisors, including specialized legal counsel and experienced CPA firms, is essential to level the playing field."*

---

## The 5-Category Due Diligence Checklist

### 1. Financial & Corporate Health
*The bedrock of valuation; expect a formal Quality of Earnings (QoE) Analysis*

| Document/Item | Details |
|---|---|
| **Financial Statements** | Income statements, balance sheets, cash flow statements for **3-5 years** + YTD monthly statements |
| **Tax Returns** | Corporate tax returns for **last 3 years** |
| **Accounts Receivable** | Detailed A/R aging reports and write-off history (low "days in A/R" is a strong positive signal) |
| **Revenue & Payer Mix** | Breakdown by CPT code, by provider, by payer (reveals revenue streams and concentration risks) |
| **Debt & Liabilities** | Schedule of all outstanding debt, loans, lines of credit |
| **Corporate Records** | Articles of Incorporation, operating agreements, shareholder lists, board meeting minutes |

### 2. Clinical & Operational Integrity
*Proves success is repeatable and scalable*

- **Patient & Visit Data:** Monthly volumes by provider/location for **24-36 months**
- **Provider Productivity:** RVUs or other metrics per provider, benchmarked against specialty standards
- **Referral Patterns:** Top referral sources showing patient pipeline stability
- **Revenue Cycle Management:** Major payer contracts, fee schedules, denial/appeals rate analysis for **12-24 months**
- **Clinical Quality:** Outcomes reports, quality metrics (e.g., MIPS), patient satisfaction surveys

### 3. Legal & Regulatory Compliance
*In healthcare, nothing sinks a deal faster than a compliance issue*

**Required Documentation:**
- ☐ All state/federal licenses, accreditations, certifications (entity + all clinical providers)
- ☐ HIPAA, OSHA, billing compliance programs + staff training records
- ☐ Summary of all past audits (CMS, commercial payers) and pending/threatened litigation
- ☐ All malpractice claims (open and closed) against practice/providers
- ☐ Adherence documentation for **Stark Law** and **Anti-Kickback Statute**

### 4. People & Contracts

- **Employee Roster:** Complete list (W2 and 1099) with role, tenure, current compensation
- **Employment Agreements:** All signed contracts (especially physicians); scrutinize **non-compete** and **non-solicit** clauses
- **Benefit Plans:** Health, retirement, and other benefit plan summaries
- **Vendor & Lease Agreements:** Significant contracts with suppliers, IT, billing companies, real estate leases/ownership

### 5. Technology & Systems
*Assesses whether tech is a scalable asset or liability requiring investment*

- **EHR/EMR:** System details, interoperability compliance, capabilities
- **IT & Software:** Inventory of critical software, license agreements, IT support contracts
- **Data Security:** Cybersecurity protocols, data backup procedures, breach history

---

## Beyond Documents: Site Visits & Interviews

Buyers will:
- Request **site visits** to observe facilities and workflow firsthand
- Conduct **interviews** with key physicians and senior administrative staff
- Assess culture, leadership, and operational realities not visible on spreadsreads

---

## Proactive Red Flag Prevention

Address these **before** due diligence begins:

| Red Flag | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|
| High provider dependence | Well-integrated associate physicians + clear succession plans |
| Messy financials | Professional cleanup + EBITDA normalization before sale |
| Lack of compliance program | Document HIPAA and billing compliance programs |
| Single-payer reliance | Diversify payer mix |
| Undisclosed regulatory issues | Early disclosure + remediation plans |

---

## Key Takeaways

- **Start preparing early** — assembling this documentation is a significant undertaking
- **Assemble your own advisory team** — specialized legal counsel and experienced CPA firms are essential
- **Transparency is critical** — proactive disclosure prevents deal-killing surprises
- **Low "days in A/R"** is explicitly called out as a strong positive signal to buyers
- **PE buyers foc

[... summary truncated for context management ...]

title: Private Equity Healthcare Deals: 2025 in Review
content:
# Private Equity Healthcare Deals: 2025 in Review

**Source:** Private Equity Stakeholder Project (PESP) | **Published:** February 11, 2026

---

## Key Data Summary

### Overall Deal Activity
- **1,029 total PE-backed healthcare deals tracked** in 2025:
  - **151** leveraged buyouts
  - **664** add-on acquisitions
  - **214** growth investments
- Involved **420 platform companies** and **708 investment firms**
- Deal count represents a slight decrease (1.9%) from 2024, attributed to methodology changes (likely an undercount)

### Global Context
- Per Bain & Company: Healthcare PE deal **value hit record high** in 2025 globally; deal volume was **second highest on record**
- North America deal volume was essentially flat year-over-year
- PE dealmaking remained steady despite **higher interest rates and tariff environment**

### Top Subsectors by Deal Activity
| Subsector | Deals |
|-----------|-------|
| Health IT | 151 |
| Dental Care | 149 |
| Outpatient Care | 148 |
| Medtech | 117 |
| Behavioral Health | 56 |

---

## Case Study: Health IT (151 Deals)

**Most active category in 2025** (second busiest in 2024 with 140 deals). Encompasses practice management software, EHR software, and revenue cycle management (RCM).

### Key Drivers
- Federal laws (2009 HITECH Act, HIPAA) intended to modernize infrastructure have fueled massive Health IT growth
- HIPAA **does not require patient consent to sell deidentified healthcare data**
- HITECH Act mandates collection of patient data → cultivated a **multibillion-dollar patient data buying/selling industry**
- **Generative AI** is accelerating growth—AI requires massive training data, making data more valuable

### Revenue Cycle Management Focus
- Per CEPR: *"As of July 2024, 199 RCM companies were PE- or VC-owned or formerly owned, including 104 current PE-backed companies."*
- PESP identified **113 PE-backed RCM companies** (December 2024)
- **47 PE deals involving RCM companies** tracked in 2025

### Notable 2025 RCM Deals
- **InTandem Capital** → HealthFuse (January, equity investment)
- **H.I.G. Capital** → GetIx Health (April, acquisition)
- **Advent International** → PatientPoint (August, acquisition)

---

## Case Study: Dental Care (149 Deals)

**95% of dental deals were add-on acquisitions** by PE-owned platform companies. **10 of the 18** platform companies with the most deals in 2025 were dental companies.

### Growth Trends
- Health Affairs study (Aug 2024): PE affiliation with dental practices **nearly doubled** from **6.6% (2015) to 12.8% (2021)**
- Highest growth in specialties: endodontists and oral surgeons (more than doubled)

### Patient Harm Concerns
- **KFF/CBS exposé (Nov 2024):** Growing trend of dentists pulling "healthy" teeth to profit from implants costing tens of thousands of dollars
- **ClearChoice** (owned by Ares Management, American Securities, Leonard Green & Partners) allegedly used salespersons ("patient education consultants") to meet patients about implants **before they saw a dentist**

### Identified Risks
PESP's 2021 report highlighted: **overtreatment, misleading advertising, and Medicaid fraud** driven by profit-driven practices.

---

## Case Study: Outpatient Care (148 Deals)

Breakdown: **119 add-on acquisitions, 7 leveraged buyouts, 22 growth/expansion investments**

### Top Specialties
- **Dermatology** and **eye care** were busiest
- **Pediatrics saw largest growth**: at least **9 investments in 2025** (3x more than 2024)

### Expert Warning
> *"...pediatrics remains a blind spot in the private equity debate. Children are the most vulnerable patients we have, and if profit driven models expand here without careful guardrails, the consequences could be severe."*
> — Thomas Kayal, Connecticut Children's Medical Center (Becker's Hospital Review, Oct 2025)

### Cost Impact Evidence
- 2023 systematic review: PE acquisition of providers associated with **higher costs for patients and payers**
- Price/cost impacts documented in: **ophthalmology, dermatology, gastroenterology, and primary care**

### Policy Response
- **Oregon and California** passed 2025 legislation to limit investor influence in physician practices

---

## Case Study: Pharma Services (42 Deals)

Includes contract research organizations, clinical trial sites, and recruitment companies. Per PitchBook (June 2024): *"Over the past two years, pharma services has become the hottest area of PE healthcare investing."*

### Investor Attraction Factors
- Global market growth driven by pharma/biotech tailwinds
- Specialty drug market complexity incentivizing outsourced services
- Highly fragmented ecosystem → consolidation opportunities
- Aging population and chronic disease increases

### Clinical Trial Deals (21 in second half of 2025)
- **Baypine** → CenExcel Clinical Research (17 sites across 7 states) (April)
- **Thomas H. Lee Partners** → Headlands Research from KKR (23 sites across U.S./Canada) (August)
- **ObjectiveHealth** (Vitruvian Partners) → Piedmont Re

[... summary truncated for context management ...]

title: Private Equity in Ambulatory Surgical Centers
content:
![Private Equity Stakeholder Project PESP](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/themes/pesp/assets//src/svg/pesp_logo.svg)
![](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/Surgery-suite-ASC.jpg)

# Private Equity in Ambulatory Surgical Centers

October 7, 2025

Michael Fenne

**Private equity firms are reshaping outpatient surgical care through consolidation, leveraged buyouts, and joint ventures with major health systems. This PESP report examines how those strategies impact costs, transparency, and competition across the fast-growing ambulatory surgery sector.**

[![](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/PESP_Report_Ambulatory-Surgical-Centers_2025-232x300.jpg)](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/PESP_Report_Ambulatory-Surgical-Centers_2025-compressed.pdf)

![](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/PESP_Report_Ambulatory-Surgical-Centers_2025-232x300.jpg)

## [Private Equity in Ambulatory Surgical Centers](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/PESP_Report_Ambulatory-Surgical-Centers_2025-compressed.pdf)

**How Wall Street’s quiet consolidation is reshaping outpatient care**

The Private Equity Stakeholder Project’s research brief, [*Private Equity in Ambulatory Surgical Centers*](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/PESP_Report_Ambulatory-Surgical-Centers_2025-compressed.pdf), examines how private equity firms have become dominant players in the $30 billion outpatient surgery market. Authored by PESP researcher **Michael Fenne**, the report traces how leveraged buyouts, joint ventures, and stealth roll-ups are transforming care delivery—and raising red flags for patients, providers, and regulators.

Ambulatory surgery centers (ASCs) now handle more than 60% of outpatient procedures in the U.S. Marketed as lower-cost, convenient alternatives to hospitals, many have instead become vehicles for Wall Street consolidation. The brief finds that private-equity-owned ASCs often **raise prices by nearly 50% within a few years**, without improving patient outcomes.

“These investments may bring many of the same risks we’ve documented across other areas of healthcare—debt burdens, short-term cost-cutting, and ownership webs that obscure accountability,” said **Michael Fenne**, senior policy researcher at PESP.

### Case Studies in Consolidation

**AmSurg / Ascension** – After a $9.9 billion leveraged buyout by KKR and subsequent bankruptcy, AmSurg was acquired by nonprofit giant Ascension for $3.9 billion—illustrating how private-equity-built networks are being absorbed by major health systems.

**Regent Surgical Health** – Backed by TowerBrook Capital and Ascension Capital, Regent forms joint-venture ASCs with nonprofits such as Mass General Brigham and the Cleveland Clinic.

**UnitedHealth / Optum** – Expanding its outpatient footprint by acquiring private-equity-assembled platforms including PE GI Solutions, OrthoAlliance, and U.S. Digestive Health.

**Surgery Partners (Bain Capital)** – The nation’s third-largest ASC operator; Bain Capital has maintained a controlling stake and sought to take the company fully private.

By the time regulators review major mergers, much of this consolidation has already taken place through smaller, unreported deals—a pattern of “stealth consolidation” that undermines competition and transparency.

### A Call for Oversight

The report concludes that stronger disclosure rules and antitrust oversight are urgently needed to monitor private equity’s expanding footprint in outpatient care. Without reform, ASCs risk becoming yet another venue where Wall Street dictates the terms of healthcare delivery—driving up costs while leaving communities with fewer choices.

[**Read the full report**](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/PESP_Report_Ambulatory-Surgical-Centers_2025-compressed.pdf)

![](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/07/42B0EEAC-7EE7-419F-8F3E-91C897EACA82.jpeg)
